observations: The component renders only text content (via `content` and an optional default slot); it has no images, icons, or other non-text content requiring a text alternative.

standard: 1.2.3 Audio Description or Media Alternative (Prerecorded) (Level A)

observations: Field description text is meant to supplement a specific form control, but this component only renders a `<div>` of hint text — it doesn't render or reference the control itself. The programmatic relationship (typically `aria-describedby` on the control pointing at this element's `id`) is the implementer's responsibility to wire up; visual proximity alone doesn't establish it for assistive technology.
This component itself is descriptive text, not an input collecting user data — the `autocomplete` attribute this criterion concerns belongs on the associated form control, not here.

Description content is communicated through text alone; the `theme`/`size` variants only change color and font size, never using color as the sole means of conveying meaning (unlike `ct-field-message`, which color-codes message type).

The `light`/`dark` theme colors (`--ct-field-description-light-color`/`--ct-field-description-dark-color`) resolve to the same body-text color tokens (`--ct-color-light-body`/`--ct-color-dark-body`) used across the design system's prose text, which meet the 4.5:1 minimum against their paired background.
Font size is set via rem-based typography tokens (`--ct-typography-text-regular-*`/`--ct-typography-text-large-*`), so text scales correctly up to 200% zoom.

The component has no borders, icons, or other UI-control chrome requiring separate contrast evaluation — it is plain text with no additional graphical elements (unlike `ct-field-message`, which pairs an icon with a colored border/background per message type).
Text is visible/readable when the line spacing is at least 1.5 times the font size, letter spacing is at least 0.12 times the font size, and word spacing is at least 0.16 times the font size.

standard: 1.4.13 Content on Hover or Focus (Level AA)

observations: The component itself renders no focusable element, but its default `<slot>` allows an implementer to place interactive content (e.g. an inline link) inside the description — the reference CivicTheme CSS explicitly styles `a:not(.ct-link)` inside `.ct-field-description` to inherit the theme's text color, anticipating this usage. Any such slotted interactive content must itself be keyboard operable; that's the implementer's responsibility since it's DOM they supply directly, not markup this component generates.

standard: 2.3.1 Three Flashes or Below Threshold (Level A)

observations: The component itself receives no focus; any slotted interactive content's position in the page's overall focus order is outside this component's scope.

If the implementer includes an inline link inside the default slot (the pattern the reference CSS explicitly supports), that link's text must be descriptive in context — this component has no way to enforce that itself.

This component supplies supplementary hint/help text, not the field's identifying label — that's `ct-label`'s responsibility. WCAG's definition of 'label' for this criterion (text or other component identifying a UI component) doesn't extend to description/hint text.
The component itself is never focusable, so it has no focus indicator of its own to evaluate; a slotted interactive element's focus-visible styling is outside this component's scope.
The component is not an interactive control with its own accessible name derived from visible text — it has no name/label binding of its own to evaluate.
The component itself is not an interactive target.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting the correct lang attribute on the html element.
Compliance depends on the implementer setting lang attributes on parts of the description text that differ from the main page language.
The component is not focusable, so it cannot itself trigger an on-focus context change.
The component has no input of its own; it is static, implementer-set text.

Compliance depends on the implementer using the same description text for the same kind of field consistently across the site (e.g. always phrasing a password-format hint the same way, not varying it page to page).
Field-level error identification is `ct-field-message`'s responsibility; `ct-field-description` renders neutral hint/help text, not error state, regardless of the field's validity.

Providing supplementary instructions/hint text for a form field beyond its label is this component's entire purpose. Once correctly associated with its control (see 1.3.1), it satisfies this criterion directly rather than leaving field-level guidance for the implementer to add separately.
Error suggestion text is `ct-field-message`'s responsibility, not this component's — `ct-field-description` is not tied to validation state.

The component renders a generic `<div>` with no implicit role beyond `generic` — it doesn't itself need a distinct name/role/value. But if the implementer relies on it to serve as an input's accessible description, they must set `aria-describedby` on the control referencing this element's `id` themselves; `ct-field-description` doesn't render the control and can't establish that link on its own.

The component is static hint text set once via `content`/slot, not a live status message; dynamic status changes are `ct-field-message`'s and/or an `aria-live` region's responsibility, outside this component's scope.
